result.claim: The study uses the CoQA dataset.
result.original_text: Using the CoQA dataset and the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model, we propose and evaluate three distinct approaches for predicting a user's next questions within a dialogue.

result.claim: The study uses the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model.
result.original_text: Using the CoQA dataset and the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model, we propose and evaluate three distinct approaches for predicting a user's next questions within a dialogue.

result.claim: The first approach identifies the dialogue's core topic and its aspects from initial user questions to guide generation.
result.original_text: The first approach identifies the dialogue's core topic and its aspects from initial user questions to guide generation.

result.claim: The second approach incorporates information from topically similar dialogues via clustering to enrich the generation context.
result.original_text: The second incorporates information from topically similar dialogues via clustering to enrich the generation context.

result.claim: The third approach bypasses explicit topic modeling, generating questions directly from the source text narrative.
result.original_text: The third bypasses explicit topic modeling, generating questions directly from the source text narrative.

result.claim: The evaluation measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions.
result.original_text: Our evaluation, which measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions, reveals that the topic-andaspect-driven first approach (EV=3,1%) outperforms both the multi-dialogue cluster-based approach (EV=2,4%) and the direct context-based approach (EV=2,4%).
result.value: expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions

result.claim: The topic-and-aspect-driven first approach has an expected value of 3,1%.

result.claim: The direct context-based approach has an expected value of 2,4%.

step3_selection.claim: The study uses the CoQA dataset.
The study uses the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model.
The first approach identifies the dialogue's core topic and its aspects from initial user questions to guide generation.
The second approach incorporates information from topically similar dialogues via clustering to enrich the generation context.
The third approach bypasses explicit topic modeling, generating questions directly from the source text narrative.
The evaluation measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions.
The topic-and-aspect-driven first approach has an expected value of 3,1%.
The multi-dialogue cluster-based approach has an expected value of 2,4%.
The direct context-based approach has an expected value of 2,4%.
The CoQA dataset contains 7199 dialogues.
The CoQA dataset contains dialogues with different numbers of questions each.
Questions in the CoQA dataset are interconnected.
Questions in the CoQA dataset often rely on context from previous turns in the dialogue.
This study specifically analyzes the subset of dialogues containing exactly 20 questions.
The LLM used in this research is Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507.
The first approach considers each dialogue separately.
To generate potential questions, the user's dialogue topic is first identified.
The questions are divided into sections: the first 60% of questions (in this case, 12) will be used to feed the model.
The remaining 40% of questions (8) will be used later as test questions.
The first 60% of questions are referred to as prompt questions (PQ).
The remaining 40% of questions are referred to as evaluate questions (EQ).
The PQ are passed to the LLM as a prompt, and the model outputs the topic of the dialogue.
The next step is to define the aspects of the identified topics.
To define aspects, the previously identified topics and the same 12 questions are fed into the LLM's prompt.
To generate questions, the identified topics, aspects, and the first 60% of questions are passed to the LLM's prompt.
The generated questions are compared with the EQ that weren't passed to the LLM's prompts.
The comparison uses a LLM-as-a-judge technique that compares each question to each other.
The comparison uses a scale from 0 to 1.
On the scale, 0.5 is defined as "Possibly equivalent (similar intent but different phrasing)".
Pairs of questions that yield a result no less than the 0.5 threshold are considered similar.
1000 dialogues were considered.
Each dialogue contains exactly 20 questions.
The topic of the dialogue was extracted using LLM.
The topics of the dialogues were grouped into clusters using DBSCAN.
The third approach eliminates the step of identifying topic and aspects of the dialogue.
Questions are generated based on the context found in each dialogue in the CoQA dataset.
The dialog context and the PQ are passed to the LLM's prompt for question generation.
The same question comparison procedure is then performed.
For approaches 1 and 3, 100 identical dialogues with 20 questions were considered.
For the second approach, a total of 85 dialogues were used.
For the second approach, dialogues were grouped into 20 clusters.
The Expected Value (EV) metric is calculated as the average percentage of test questions per dialogue that can be predicted.
The total number of Expected Questions (EQ) per dialogue is 8.
Approach 1 (Topic and aspects based question generation) has an Expected Value (EV) of 3,1.
Approach 2 (Domain and aspects based question generation) has an Expected Value (EV) of 2,4.
Approach 3 (Context-based generation from user questions) has an Expected Value (EV) of 2,4.
